item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed two minor mistakes (double "the" and putting 3367-2 when it was referring to 3367). Forgot to write the comment.

Kinch, this is fucking awesome. I don't really have much else to say besides that. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s so cool that something I wrote got featured. This is probably one of u more personal scps. A lot less personal than 2420 tho.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quited like this one! Gave me a haunting feeling that was consistent throughout the read as it started and ended, and the different perspectives added a differentiating "texture" if that makes sense to the entire story and how even an MTF Commander got changed by the place.

+1

Not sure why the logo is still the same from June, tho. probs just authors choice to leave it in,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i put it down to "everything is canon in its own frame of reference"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can’t the discrepancy with the number of O5 members be explained by the passage of time, with members being added and dying/leaving through various means? I don’t think there’s anything that says there always has to be the same number.

Edit: Sorry, misunderstood slightly. Same applies for the Ethics Committee though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is haunting and the differing viewpoints are interesting; +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Doulworth County, Ohio

What is it with people on this site making up fake counties in Ohio? :V

Bunch of small things kept me from voting, like pink and blue how? Or the fact that you don't introduce "E-65" until the second interview, which was confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

There is an issue that should be fixed. In "SCP-3367 generally manifest naked and appear to be undergoing extreme gastrointestinal distress." SCP-3367 should be SCP-3367-1.

Also: "In a 13-0 decision, the Ethics Committee has deemed D-Class testing of SCP-3367 to be unethical and of no material value to the Foundation." I'd like to say that the Ethics Committee does not have 13 people, but then I looked around and apparently it's not set in stone? There are articles that have 4+, 5, 9, and 13 members. I really wish this discrepancy was explained, but whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot of reasons I love this. I think my one complaint might be that it could have gotten where it was going a little slower through implicit story-telling instead of explicit story-telling (exploration logs, maybe?), but the characterizations are compelling enough. There's a lot of little touches throughout this that I really adore (such as SCP-3367-1 continuing to sing hymns for so long).

The subject you're dealing with is obviously quite sensitive, and that's another reason I appreciate this: It touches that subject while avoiding both being preachy and sensationalist.

(Okay, some elements are pretty sensationalistic, but I don't think those elements go over the top or detract from the piece as a whole)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like I said in the IRC chat, this hits home and hits hard, and makes me feel a very peculiar and particular emotion that I can't really describe.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this! It’s everything I love in SCPs like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was a bit confused about a few things at first, but the final interview tied it all up in a neat little bow. Extremely well written piece.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really spoke to me. Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow this was a good read. Excellent job mood setting and crafting a tragic narrative. I think some more clarification on how the non-euclidean architecture "consumes" 3367-1 would be nice though. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This kinda rage don't just boil over. And it's eternal catharsis. That's what it is. Shit almost makes me jealous.

This is the type of writing I love the site for. There's a ton of characterization and really deep exploration of themes packed into a small space here, with room left over for a monster story that's good at face value. Cannot +1 hard enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoy the differing perspectives from two people who would have particular insight into the issue on if SCP-3367-2 is happy or not. It's almost like, uh, like Alfa and Myron, though they both passed up the offer to join, weren't able to move on from the situation in the same way and that's not down to what treatment they received after the fact but what they saw in that moment of deciding to turn away. Myron, who already felt free for having left, and freer for leaving again, saw children locked in eternal non-progress, wanted nothing of it. Alfa, who thought their way out of having seen this was death, saw children locked in eternal triumph, and, sure, wasn't ready to become… that, but.

I don't know. It felt like even without remembering, at the end, Alfa was drawn back in to the site of the triumph she never had, and couldn't resist it twice. I'm not sure if that was the intended reading or not, but it was how I read it, and I enjoyed that. I really enjoyed the level of contrast in the two interviews, either way. I feel this is a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"A bottomless curse, a bottomless sea, accepting of all that there is and can be."

More 2 Come.
